|last5=Tovée |first5=Martin J. |title=Weight-Based Discrimination in Occupational Hiring and Helping Behavior |journal=Journal of Applied Social Psychology |date=April 2008 |volume=38 |issue=4 |pages=968–981 |doi=10.1111/j.1559-1816.2008.00334.x }}</ref> The negative treatment of overweight and obese individuals may stem from beliefs that weight is controllable and those who fail to control their weight are lazy, unmotivated, and lack self-discipline.<ref name="Greenleaf">{{cite journal |last1=Greenleaf |first1=Christy |last2=Starks |first2=Misty |last3=Gomez |first3=Laura |last4=Chambliss |first4=Heather |last5=Martin |first5=Scott |title=Weight-related words associated with figure silhouettes |journal=Body Image |date=December 2004 |volume=1 |issue=4 |pages=373–384 |doi=10.1016/j.bodyim.2004.10.004 |pmid=18089167 }}</ref><ref name="Bellizzi">{{cite journal |last1=Bellizzi |first1=Joseph A. |last2=Hasty |first2=Ronald W. |title=Territory Assignment Decisions and Supervising Unethical Selling Behavior: The Effects of Obesity and Gender as Moderated by Job-Related Factors |journal=Journal of Personal Selling & Sales Management |date=1 January 1998 |volume=18 |issue=2 |pages=35–49 |doi=10.1080/08853134.1998.10754129 |id={{ProQuest|216749832}} |jstor=40471680 |s2cid=151726125 }}</ref> Underweight individuals may also be subject to appearance-related negative treatment.<ref name="Swami"/> Underweight, overweight and obese applicants are not protected from discrimination by any current United States laws.<ref name="Roehling"/> However, some individuals who are morbidly obese and whose obesity is due to a physiological disorder may be protected against discrimination under the Americans with Disabilities Act.<ref name="King">{{cite journal |last1=King |first1=Eden B. |last2=Shapiro |first2=Jenessa R. |last3=Hebl |first3=Michelle R. |last4=Singletary |first4=Sarah L. |last5=Turner |first5=Stacey |title=The stigma of obesity in customer service: A mechanism for remediation and bottom-line consequences of interpersonal discrimination. |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_journal-of-applied-psychology_2006-05_91_3/page/579 |journal=Journal of Applied Psychology |date=2006 |volume=91 |issue=3 |pages=579–593 |doi=10.1037/0021-9010.91.3.579 |pmid=16737356 }}</ref>

Discrimination against pregnant applicants is illegal under the Pregnancy Discrimination Act of 1978, which views pregnancy as a temporary disability and requires employers to treat pregnant applicants the same as all other applicants.<ref name="US">{{cite web |title=U.S. Equal Employment Opportunity Commission (2011) Pregnancy discrimination |url=http://www.eeoc.gov/laws/types/pregnancy.cfm |publisher=Eeoc.gov }}</ref> Yet, discrimination against pregnant applicants continues both in the United States and internationally.<ref name="US"/><ref name="Gatrell">{{cite journal |last1=Gatrell |first1=Caroline |title=Managing the Maternal Body: A Comprehensive Review and Transdisciplinary Analysis: Managing the Maternal Body |journal=International Journal of Management Reviews |date=March 2011 |volume=13 |issue=1 |pages=97–112 |doi=10.1111/j.1468-2370.2010.00286.x |s2cid=142958889 |doi-access=free }}</ref> Research shows that pregnant applicants compared to non-pregnant applicants are less likely to be recommended for hire.<ref name="Bragger">{{cite journal |last1=Bragger |first1=Jennifer DeNicolis |last2=Kutcher |first2=Eugene |last3=Morgan |first3=John |last4=Firth |first4=Patricia |title=The Effects of the Structured Interview on Reducing Biases Against Pregnant Job Applicants |journal=Sex Roles |date=2002 |volume=46 |issue=7/8 |pages=215–226 |doi=10.1023/A:1019967231059 |s2cid=141006365 }}</ref><ref name="Cunningham">{{cite journal |last1=Cunningham |first1=Jennifer |last2=Macan |first2=Therese |title=Effects of Applicant Pregnancy on Hiring Decisions and Interview Ratings |journal=Sex Roles |date=19 September 2007 |volume=57 |issue=7–8 |pages=497–508 |doi=10.1007/s11199-007-9279-0 |s2cid=145686325 }}</ref> Interviewers appear concerned that pregnant applicants are more likely than non-pregnant applicants to miss work and even quit.<ref name="Cunningham"/> Organizations who wish to reduce potential discrimination against pregnant applicants should consider implementing structured interviews, although some theoretical work suggests interviewers may still show biases even in these types of interviews.<ref name="Bragger"/><ref>{{cite book |doi=10.1002/9781119992592.ch8 |chapter=Actions Speak Too: Uncovering Possible Implicit and Explicit Discrimination in the Employment Interview Process |title=International Review of Industrial and Organizational Psychology 2011 |year=2011 |last1=Macan |first1=Therese |last2=Merritt |first2=Stephanie |pages=293–337 |isbn=978-1-119-99259-2 }}</ref>

=== Other forms of discrimination ===

Employers are using social networking sites like Facebook and LinkedIn to obtain additional information about job applicants.<ref name="Cross">{{cite web |title=Cross-Tab (2010). Online reputation in a connected world |url=http://www.microsoft.com/privacy/dpd/research.aspx |publisher=Microsoft.com |archive-url=https://archive.today/20100411190004/http://www.microsoft.com/privacy/dpd/research.aspx |archive-date=April 11, 2010 |access-date=May 9, 2011 |url-status=live }}</ref><ref name="JobVite">{{cite web |publisher=[[Jobvite]]|year=2010 |title=Jobvite: 2010 social recruiting survey results |url=http://recruiting.jobvite.com/resources/social-recruiting-survey.php }}</ref><ref name="SHRM">{{cite book |year=2007 |title=2007 Advances in E-recruiting: Leveraging the .jobs Domain |publisher=Society for Human Resource Management |url=https://search.alexanderstreet.com/preview/work/bibliographic_entity%7Cbibliographic_details%7C2413344 |oclc=891140235 }}</ref> While these sites may be useful to verify resume information, profiles with pictures also may reveal much more information about the applicant, including issues pertaining to applicant weight and pregnancy.<ref name="Grasz">{{cite press release |title=Forty-five Percent of Employers Use Social Networking Sites to Research Job Candidates, CareerBuilder Survey Finds |publisher=CareerBuilder |date=19 August 2009 |url=http://press.careerbuilder.com/2009-08-19-Forty-five-Percent-of-Employers-Use-Social-Networking-Sites-to-Research-Job-Candidates-CareerBuilder-Survey-Finds |access-date=September 1, 2020 }}</ref> Some employers are also asking potential job candidates for their social media logins which has alarmed many privacy watch dogs and regulators.<ref name="Fox News">{{cite news |title=Senators call for federal probe over employers asking for Facebook passwords |url=https://www.foxnews.com/politics/senators-call-for-federal-probe-over-employers-asking-for-facebook-passwords/ |work=Fox News |agency=Associated Press |date=26 March 2015 }}</ref>

Although this article does discuss some issues of job applicant discrimination, there could be many more types and factors of discrimination in the workplace than noted here. The most common types of discrimination within the workplace are ethnic and gender/sexual orientation discrimination. In an experiment performed in the US by Marianne Bertrand and Sendhil Mullainathan, it was noted that job applicants "with white-sounding names got 50 percent more callbacks for interviews than those with African-American-sounding names" <ref>{{Citation|title=Employment discrimination|date=2020-11-17|url=https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Employment_discrimination&oldid=989214590|work=Wikipedia|language=en|access-date=2020-11-27}}</ref> This shows that something as simple as a person's name could be the reason they do or do not get a chance to have a job interview.

<!-- Deleted image removed: [[File:Frontiers Identifying Moderators in the Link Between Workplace Discrimination and Health-Well-Being.png|thumb]] -->
Another note to add to this article, is the idea of how discrimination within a workplace/job interview can affect a person's health and wellbeing. A person that is looking to find a job, no matter the industry, should not have to worry about whether or not they are a good candidate because of what discrimination might be placed against them. "Perceived discrimination can cause a lot of stress to an individual" <ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Xu|first1=Yue Ethel|last2=Chopik|first2=William J.|date=2020|title=Identifying Moderators in the Link Between Workplace Discrimination and Health/Well-Being|journal=Frontiers in Psychology|language=en|volume=11|article-number=458|doi=10.3389/fpsyg.2020.00458|pmid=32256433|pmc=7092632|issn=1664-1078|doi-access=free}}</ref> which in turn could make it more difficult for a person to get job/job interview.

==Cross-cultural issues==
As with the common comparisons between Eastern and Western cultures, interviews and the [[Construct (philosophy)|constructs]] assessed by the interview have been found to differ across the world. For example, studies of the United States of America (USA) to Canada have found conflicting results in average levels of agreeableness in each country.<ref name=":0">{{cite journal |last1=Heine |first1=Steven J. |last2=Buchtel |first2=Emma E. |last3=Norenzayan |first3=Ara |title=What Do Cross-National Comparisons of Personality Traits Tell Us?: The Case of Conscientiousness |journal=Psychological Science |date=April 2008 |volume=19 |issue=4 |pages=309–313 |doi=10.1111/j.1467-9280.2008.02085.x |pmid=18399880 |s2cid=3828183 }}</ref> People tend to use social comparison when reporting their own level of agreeableness.<ref name=":0" /> Even though Canadians are likely to be more agreeable, they might score similarly to those individuals from the USA.<ref name=":0" /> In situations where social comparison is a factor, an honest answer could result in under- or over-estimation.

Because of these cultural differences, more businesses are adding cross-cultural training to their HR training.<ref name=":1">{{cite journal |last1=Lievens |first1=Filip |last2=Harris |first2=Michael M. |last3=Van Keer |first3=Etienne |last4=Bisqueret |first4=Claire |title=Predicting cross-cultural training performance: The validity of personality, cognitive ability, and dimensions measured by an assessment center and a behavior description interview. |journal=Journal of Applied Psychology |date=2003 |volume=88 |issue=3 |pages=476–489 |doi=10.1037/0021-9010.88.3.476 |pmid=12814296 |s2cid=37456091 |url=https://ink.library.smu.edu.sg/lkcsb_research/5657 }}</ref><ref>{{cite book |last1=Sinangil |first1=H.K. |last2=Ones |first2=D.S. |year=2001 |chapter=Expatriate management: Personnel psychology |editor1-last=Anderson |editor1-first=N. |editor2-last=Ones |editor2-first=D.S. |editor3-last=Sinangil |editor3-first=H.K. |editor4-last=Viswesvaran |editor4-first=C. |title=Handbook of Industrial, Work and Organizational Psychology |url=https://archive.org/details/handbookindustri02ande |publisher=Sage |pages=[https://archive.org/details/handbookindustri02ande/page/n452 424]–443 }}</ref> The goal of cross-cultural training is to improve one's ability to adapt and judge people from other cultures. This training is a first step in ensuring the process of using the job interview to decide whom to hire works the same in a selection situation where there are cross-cultural factors.

One cultural difference in the job interview is in the type of questions applicants will expect and not expect to be asked.<ref name=":2">{{cite journal |last1=Posthuma |first1=Richard A. |last2=Levashina |first2=Julia |last3=Lievens |first3=Filip |last4=Schollaert |first4=Eveline |last5=Tsai |first5=Wei-Chi |last6=Wagstaff |first6=Maria Fernanda |last7=Campion |first7=Michael A. |title=Comparing employment interviews in Latin America with other countries |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_journal-of-business-research_2014-05_67_5/page/943 |journal=Journal of Business Research |date=May 2014 |volume=67 |issue=5 |pages=943–951 |doi=10.1016/j.jbusres.2013.07.014 |s2cid=8935071 }}</ref> Interviewers outside the USA often ask about family, marital status, and children.<ref name=":2" /> These types of questions are usually not allowed by USA job laws but are acceptable in other countries. Applicants can be surprised by questions interviewers ask them that are not appropriate or consistent with their own cultures. For example, in Belgium and Russia, interviewers are unlikely to ask about an applicant's personal values, opinions and beliefs.<ref name=":2" /> Thus, USA interviewers who do ask applicants about their values can make non-USA applicants uneasy or misinterpret the reason they are not prepared.

Another difference is in the consistency with which common constructs, even those that generalize across cultures, predict across different countries and cultures. For example, those who seem high in [[Big Five personality traits#Agreeableness|Agreeableness]] can do less well on the job in European workplaces.<ref name=":1" /> But those high in Agreeableness in the USA or Japan will do better on the job as measured on the same criteria.<ref name=":1" /> In some cases the structured Behavior Description Interview (BDI) that predicts who will do well on the job in some countries, from their interview scores, fails to predict accurately which applicants to hire in other countries.<ref name=":1" />

===Methodological biases===
====Construct bias====
There are a few ways that cross-cultural differences can mess up the results of our attempts to predict job performance.<ref name=":3">{{cite journal |last1=van de Vijver |first1=Fons J. R. |last2=Poortinga |first2=Ype H. |title=Towards an Integrated Analysis of Bias in Cross-Cultural Assessment |journal=European Journal of Psychological Assessment |date=January 1997 |volume=13 |issue=1 |pages=29–37 |doi=10.1027/1015-5759.13.1.29 |url=https://pure.uvt.nl/ws/files/222002/26699_11820.pdf }}</ref> The first source of error is construct bias, the possibility that the construct being measured is viewed differently by those from another culture if it exists at all. One way this could happen is if the behaviors a person displays, that go with that construct, are viewed differently in different cultures. It could also be the extent to which the construct even exists in their country. For example, the Multidimensional Work Ethic Profile (MWEP), is a scale demonstrated to work across many countries.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Lim |first1=Doo H. |last2=Woehr |first2=David J. |last3=You |first3=Yeong Mahn |last4=Allen Gorman |first4=C. |title=The translation and development of a short form of the Korean language version of the multidimensional work ethic profile |journal=Human Resource Development International |date=September 2007 |volume=10 |issue=3 |pages=319–331 |doi=10.1080/13678860701515406 |s2cid=44203819 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Woehr |first1=David J. |last2=Arciniega |first2=Luis M. |last3=Lim |first3=Doo H. |title=Examining Work Ethic Across Populations: A Comparison of the Multidimensional Work Ethic Profile Across Three Diverse Cultures |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_educational-and-psychological-measurement_2007-02_67_1/page/154 |journal=Educational and Psychological Measurement |date=February 2007 |volume=67 |issue=1 |pages=154–168 |doi=10.1177/0013164406292036 |s2cid=143045918 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Yousef |first1=Darwish A. |title=Islamic work ethic – A moderator between organizational commitment and job satisfaction in a cross-cultural context |journal=Personnel Review |date=April 2001 |volume=30 |issue=2 |pages=152–169 |doi=10.1108/00483480110380325 }}</ref> However, in China the MWEP concept/dimension of Leisure has been shown to have poor equivalence with other countries, and may be a culturally inappropriate assessment due to the Confucian concept of hard work without leisure.<ref>{{cite thesis |last1=Chen |first1=Danxia |title=Eastern Work Ethic: Structural Validity, Measurement Invariance, and Generational Differences |url=https://digital.library.unt.edu/ark:/67531/metadc500082/ }}</ref> Research has shown that differences in the levels of established cross-cultural constructs such as [[Michele J. Gelfand#Cultural tightness-looseness|Cultural Tightness-Looseness]] increase or decrease the effect of the Five Factor Model personality traits.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Shao |first1=Lian |last2=Webber |first2=Sheila |title=A cross-cultural test of the 'five-factor model of personality and transformational leadership' |journal=Journal of Business Research |date=August 2006 |volume=59 |issue=8 |pages=936–944 |doi=10.1016/j.jbusres.2006.02.005 |url=https://spectrum.library.concordia.ca/1557/1/MQ83965.pdf }}</ref> Tight cultures have strong social norms and adherence coupled with low tolerance for behavior that deviates from those norms, and loose cultures are the opposite with weak norms and high tolerance for deviance.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Gelfand |first1=M. J. |last2=Raver |first2=J. L. |last3=Nishii |first3=L. |last4=Leslie |first4=L. M. |last5=Lun |first5=J. |last6=Lim |first6=B. C. |last7=Duan |first7=L. |last8=Almaliach |first8=A. |last9=Ang |first9=S. |last10=Arnadottir |first10=J. |last11=Aycan |first11=Z. |last12=Boehnke |first12=K. |last13=Boski |first13=P. |last14=Cabecinhas |first14=R. |last15=Chan |first15=D. |last16=Chhokar |first16=J. |last17=D'Amato |first17=A. |last18=Ferrer |first18=M. |last19=Fischlmayr |first19=I. C. |last20=Fischer |first20=R. |last21=Fulop |first21=M. |last22=Georgas |first22=J. |last23=Kashima |first23=E. S. |last24=Kashima |first24=Y. |last25=Kim |first25=K. |last26=Lempereur |first26=A. |last27=Marquez |first27=P. |last28=Othman |first28=R. |last29=Overlaet |first29=B. |last30=Panagiotopoulou |first30=P. |last31=Peltzer |first31=K. |last32=Perez-Florizno |first32=L. R. |last33=Ponomarenko |first33=L. |last34=Realo |first34=A. |last35=Schei |first35=V. |last36=Schmitt |first36=M. |last37=Smith |first37=P. B. |last38=Soomro |first38=N. |last39=Szabo |first39=E. |last40=Taveesin |first40=N. |last41=Toyama |first41=M. |last42=Van de Vliert |first42=E. |last43=Vohra |first43=N. |last44=Ward |first44=C. |last45=Yamaguchi |first45=S. |title=Differences Between Tight and Loose Cultures: A 33-Nation Study |journal=Science |date=27 May 2011 |volume=332 |issue=6033 |pages=1100–1104 |doi=10.1126/science.1197754 |pmid=21617077 |bibcode=2011Sci...332.1100G |hdl=1813/75456 |s2cid=18083852 |url=https://digitalcommons.ilr.cornell.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=2302&context=articles |hdl-access=free }}</ref> An interviewer from a tight culture might view the normal behaviors of a loose cultured interviewee as signs of a poor moral character despite the behavior being normal. As such, differences between the tightness-looseness of the interviewer's and interviewee's home countries can introduce method bias, negatively affecting the interviewer's assessment of the interviewee's answers and behaviors. First construct bias must be measured by comparing groups of persons from distinct cultures and comparing if any real differences are discovered. The information on those differences can be used to make the adjustments needed to allow the construct to measure what it is intended to measure in people from different cultures.

====Method bias====
[[Response bias]] is another cross-cultural difference that has been shown to affect how we measure constructs and interpret the results.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Fischer |first1=Ronald |title=Standardization to Account for Cross-Cultural Response Bias: A Classification of Score Adjustment Procedures and Review of Research in JCCP |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_journal-of-cross-cultural-psychology_2004-05_35_3/page/263 |journal=Journal of Cross-Cultural Psychology |date=May 2004 |volume=35 |issue=3 |pages=263–282 |doi=10.1177/0022022104264122 |s2cid=32046329 }}</ref> [[Social desirability bias]] is a tendency to give a socially acceptable answer, even if it is a lie because we want to look good. Giving socially acceptable, but partly or completely false, answers can inflate interview scores.<ref name=":4">{{cite journal |last1=Odendaal |first1=Aletta |title=Cross-cultural differences in social desirability scales: Influence of cognitive ability |journal=SA Journal of Industrial Psychology |date=5 February 2015 |volume=41 |issue=1 |page=13|doi=10.4102/sajip.v41i1.1259 |doi-access=free }}</ref> One simple example of socially acceptable answers is called acquiescence bias, which is a tendency to agree with all questions with a positive meaning.<ref name=":4" /> People also have been found to show different attitudes towards answers on the extreme high and low end of a set of options (extremely agree or extremely disagree).<ref name=":4" /> In some cases, people from different cultures may just be unfamiliar with a word (term, concept, context) or with a type of question.<ref name=":4" /> Another research study found that self and other reports of conscientiousness failed to relate with expected job behaviors across cultures, demonstrating that one of the most predictive constructs in the USA is tied to aspects of USA culture that may not be present in a different type of culture.<ref name=":0" />

For example, in the West, applicants prefer to eliminate details and focus on the larger issue, tending towards a comprehensive evaluation starting from individual elements and then moving towards the whole.<ref name=":5">{{cite book |last1=Lievens |first1=Filip |chapter=International situational judgment tests |pages=279–300 |chapter-url=https://ink.library.smu.edu.sg/lkcsb_research/5813 |editor1-last=Weekley |editor1-first=Jeff A. |editor2-last=Ployhart |editor2-first=Robert E. |year=2006 |title=Situational Judgment Tests: Theory, Measurement, and Application |publisher=Lawrence Erlbaum Associates |isbn=978-0-8058-5251-6 }}</ref> In Japan, a respondent would go from the general to the specific in answering, preferring to divide a problem and analyze it piece by piece. Likewise, there are differences between individualist and collectivist cultures in the types of answers they chose. When given a series of options, individualists tend to choose the task-oriented option that involves direct communication with others.<ref name=":5" /> Yet collectivists choose the option that sees group harmony and protecting or saving face for others as more important.<ref name=":5" /> These differences can introduce method bias when interviewers evaluate or score how the applicant did in the interview. This is why it is important to understand how and why the best answer in one culture is not the best elsewhere. It might even be completely wrong.

====Item bias====
There is also item bias introduced by the actual items or questions in an interview. Poor item translation can be a problem.<ref name=":3" /> This might be incorrectly translating the same item to another language such as in an organization that hires both English and Spanish-speaking employees. Or it might be in someone not understanding the wording of an item because they are not native to that country's language. Similar to construct bias, the wording of an item can result in measuring different traits because of different meanings in the two different cultures.

==See also==
* [[College interview]]
* [[Job hunting]]

{{Jobs}}
{{Authority control}}

[[Category:Job interview| ]]
[[Category:Recruitment]]
[[Category:Industrial and organizational psychology]]
